---
toc: false
listing:
  - id: personal-posts
    contents: "../posts/*/index.qmd"
    sort: "date desc"
    max-items: 2
    include:
      categories: "journal"
    type: default
    fields: [title, date, description]
---

```{=html}
<!-- Photo + first two paragraphs side by side -->
<div style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: 230px 1fr; gap: 2rem; align-items: start; margin-bottom: 0;">
  <img src="../images/erwin-and-the-bobos.jpg"
       style="width: 230px; height: 420px; object-fit: cover; object-position: center 15%; border-radius: 8px; display: block;"
       alt="Erwin Lares with his dogs">
  <div>
    <p style="font-size:1rem; line-height:1.85; margin-bottom:1.25rem;">I grew up on a farm in Venezuela. The part I remember most is called <em>las rubieras</em> &mdash; a stretch of land that stays impossibly green through the dry season, a testament to my dad's ingenuity. I didn't know then that I'd name a pottery studio after it, or that I'd spend most of my adult life trying to understand why people reach for metaphors when plain language would do just fine.</p>
    <p style="font-size:1rem; line-height:1.85; margin-bottom:1.25rem;">I studied electrical engineering at Universidad Simón Bolívar, which says something about the path I was on at twenty. Circuits and systems challenged me, but they never really captured my imagination. Coding did. Little did I know that after a 20-year hiatus, my professional life would bring me back to that early interest.</p>
  </div>
</div>

<!-- Remaining paragraphs at full width -->
<div style="margin-top: 1.25rem;">
  <p style="font-size:1rem; line-height:1.85; margin-bottom:1.25rem;">I wandered quite a bit, though. I moved to the United States and started teaching Spanish, first in rural Illinois and eventually at UW–Madison. Language stopped being something I used and became something I studied. I started my PhD in linguistics in my forties and defended in 2022, which felt late until I realized that is roughly when you have enough life to find idioms genuinely interesting.</p>
<p>My dissertation was about idiomatic phrases — the kind where the words don't mean what they mean. Kick the bucket. Estirar la pata. What fascinated me was that listeners understand these phrases without being explicitly told: something in the signal gives them away. I've never been able to fully let go of that question, and it turns out it has followed me into stranger places than I expected.</p>
<p>These days I work as a Research Data Science Consultant with Research Cyberinfrastructure at UW–Madison, helping researchers use tools and strategies that make their work more reproducible, shareable, and visible. I also help run BRUG, an R users community for Badgers. It turns out that teaching people to think about language and teaching people to think about data have more in common than you might expect — both are about learning to ask better questions.</p>
<p>The pottery started as a way to be in my body after too many hours at a desk. There's something clarifying about a material that doesn't negotiate — clay does what physics tells it to do. I also practice and teach Aikido in Janesville, which is another discipline built on the premise that force isn't always the answer.</p>
<p>I'm a humanist. I believe in fairness, in access, and in the idea that good tools should be in everyone's hands. I have a dog. I drink a lot of coffee. I speak Spanish natively and English as an L2 speaker, which means I notice things monolingual speakers miss — and occasionally miss things they'd catch immediately.</p>
</div>

<hr class="about-divider">
```

## Journal

::: {#journal-posts}
:::
